Clinical Training

Ibn Sina | Faculty of Medicine | Fourth Year |Radilogy /Abha Center

LECTURE 2

Chest X-Ray Masterclass

Build a systematic, reproducible approach to reading every CXR you encounter — from quality check to structured report.

ABCDE System RIE Quality Check Real X-Ray Images 5 Pathologies

Administrative Check — Every Time

  • Patient identity: Name and date of birth — correct patient's film?
  • Date: Most recent film? Compare with prior imaging if available
  • Projection: PA, AP, lateral? Erect or supine?
  • Clinical question: Why was this ordered? Post-procedural? Dyspnoea? Admission?
Clinical Insight

Context changes what you look for. A post-central-line film demands scrutiny for pneumothorax and line position. Never read a CXR in isolation from the clinical history.

R
Rotation
I
Inspiration
E
Exposure

R — Rotation

  • Medial clavicle ends equidistant from spinous process
  • Rotation distorts the mediastinum and can falsely deviate the trachea

I — Inspiration

  • Adequate = 6 anterior ribs or 10 posterior ribs above the diaphragm
  • Poor inspiration → apparent cardiomegaly and basal haziness

E — Exposure

  • Correct: vertebral bodies just visible behind the heart
  • Over-penetrated: too dark — pathology missed
  • Under-penetrated: too white — poor contrast
Clinical Insight

A technically poor film can mimic pathology. Identify all RIE issues before reporting any findings.

AirBlack
FatDark grey
Soft tissueGrey
BoneWhite
MetalBright
← Radiolucent (dark)    Radiodense (bright) →

Clinical Application

  • Air (black): lungs, trachea, bowel — pathological air = pneumothorax, pneumoperitoneum
  • Soft tissue/Water (grey): heart, effusions, tumours — all same shade
  • Bone (white): always assess ribs, clavicles, and spine
Exam Insight

The silhouette sign — loss of the right heart border = right middle lobe pathology.

A
B
C
D
E

ABCDE Walkthrough — Normal Film

  • A — Airway: Trachea midline. Carina angle normal. Bronchi patent.
  • B — Breathing: Both lung fields clear. Lung markings to periphery. No opacity, PTX, or effusion.
  • C — Cardiac: CTR <0.5. All borders well-defined. Mediastinum not widened.
  • D — Diaphragm: Right higher than left. Costophrenic angles sharp. No free air.
  • E — Extras: No rib fractures. No soft tissue swelling. No lines or tubes.

Trachea

  • Normal: midline or slightly right at the level of the clavicles
  • Deviation toward abnormal side: collapse or fibrosis (pull)
  • Deviation away from abnormal side: tension pneumothorax, large effusion (push)

Carina

  • Normal angle: ≤ 70°
  • Widening: left atrial enlargement or subcarinal lymphadenopathy

Main Bronchi

  • Right main bronchus: shorter, wider, more vertical
  • ETT tip: 5 cm above the carina
Clinical Insight

Deviated trachea + absent breath sounds + haemodynamic instability = tension pneumothorax. Do not wait for imaging — needle decompress immediately.

Lung Zones

  • Divide each lung into upper, mid, and lower zones — compare both sides simultaneously
  • Lung markings should be visible to the periphery — absent = pneumothorax
  • Left hilum normally slightly higher than the right

Four Patterns to Recognise

  • Consolidation: opacity without volume loss + air bronchograms
  • Collapse: opacity with volume loss + mediastinal shift toward
  • Effusion: blunted CP angle + meniscus sign
  • Pneumothorax: absent peripheral markings + visceral pleural line
Clinical Insight

Compare both lung fields simultaneously, zone by zone. Your eye detects asymmetry faster than absolute assessment.

Cardiothoracic Ratio (CTR)

  • CTR = maximum cardiac width ÷ maximum thoracic width
  • Normal: ≤ 0.5 on an erect PA film only
  • CTR > 0.5 = cardiomegaly — only valid on PA film

Cardiac Borders

  • Right border: right atrium
  • Left border (top→bottom): aortic knuckle → pulmonary trunk → left atrial appendage → left ventricle
  • Loss of border = silhouette sign
Exam Insight

Never assess CTR on an AP film. Write: "Heart appears enlarged on this AP projection — technical magnification cannot be excluded."

Normal Diaphragm

  • Right hemidiaphragm higher by up to 2.5 cm (liver beneath)
  • Costophrenic angles acute and sharp
  • Gastric bubble visible below left hemidiaphragm

Abnormal Findings

  • Blunted CP angle: ≥ 200 mL pleural fluid
  • Free air under diaphragm: pneumoperitoneum — surgical emergency
  • Elevated hemidiaphragm: phrenic nerve palsy, lower lobe collapse
  • Flat diaphragm: hyperinflation — COPD, acute asthma

Lines and Tubes — Key Positions

  • ETT: tip 5 cm above carina
  • NGT: must cross diaphragm and enter gastric fundus
  • CVC: tip at SVC–right atrial junction
  • ICC drain: apex for pneumothorax; base for effusion

Bones and Soft Tissues

  • Ribs: fractures, lytic lesions, rib notching (coarctation)
  • Surgical emphysema: linear air streaking in subcutaneous tissues
  • Absent breast shadow: may indicate mastectomy — not lung pathology
Clinical Insight

A misplaced NGT in the lung used for feed delivery is a serious preventable patient safety incident. Check every line on every post-procedural film.

Patient: ____________ | Date: ____________ | Projection: PA / AP
Quality: Rotation ___  Inspiration ___  Exposure ___
A: Trachea ______. Carina ______. Bronchi ______.
B: R lung ______. L lung ______. Hila ______. Pleurae ______.
C: CTR ___. Borders ______. Mediastinum ______.
D: R ______. L ______. CPA angles ______. Subdiaphragmatic ______.
E: Bones ______. Soft tissues ______. Lines/tubes ______.
Impression: ______________________________.

Developed by Dr. Salaheldin Terair · 2026

Tap each condition to reveal its radiological features on a real X-ray image.

Consolidation
Pneumonia · Oedema · Haemorrhage
▼
Left lower zone consolidation — homogeneous opacity, no volume loss

Definition

  • Alveoli filled with fluid, pus, blood, or cells — displacing air

Key CXR Features

  • Homogeneous opacity — lobar or segmental distribution
  • Air bronchograms — pathognomonic; visible air-filled bronchi within opacity
  • No volume loss — lung retains normal size
  • Silhouette sign: loss of adjacent cardiac or diaphragm border

Causes

  • Pneumonia (most common), pulmonary oedema, haemorrhage, aspiration
Clinical Insight

Air bronchograms confirm consolidation — they are NOT seen in pleural effusion or collapse. This is the key distinguishing sign.

Lobar Collapse
Atelectasis · Volume Loss
▼
Lobar collapse — note volume loss and mediastinal shift

Key CXR Features

  • Opacification with volume loss — key distinction from consolidation
  • Ipsilateral mediastinal shift toward affected side
  • Elevated hemidiaphragm on affected side
  • Crowding of ribs on affected side

Lobar Patterns

  • RML collapse: loss of right heart border (silhouette sign)
  • LUL collapse: veil-like haze; Luftsichel sign
  • LLL collapse: triangular opacity behind heart (sail sign)
Clinical Insight

Collapse deviates mediastinum TOWARD — effusion deviates it AWAY.

Pleural Effusion
CCF · Infection · Malignancy
▼
Right pleural effusion — blunted costophrenic angle, meniscus sign

Key CXR Features

  • Blunted costophrenic angle — earliest sign (≥ 200 mL)
  • Meniscus sign — concave upper border, higher laterally
  • Homogeneous lower hemithorax opacity
  • Massive effusion: contralateral mediastinal shift

Causes

  • Cardiac failure (bilateral), pneumonia, malignancy, PE, hepatic/renal failure
Clinical Insight

Massive effusion with no mediastinal shift = consider underlying collapse or mesothelioma.

Pneumothorax
Simple · Tension · Spontaneous
▼
Right pneumothorax — visceral pleural line and absent peripheral lung markings

Key CXR Features

  • Absent lung markings in the peripheral zone
  • Visceral pleural line — thin white line separated from chest wall
  • Peripheral lucency lateral to the lung edge

Tension — Emergency Signs

  • Tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side
  • Mediastinal shift AWAY from affected side
  • Ipsilateral hemidiaphragm depressed
Clinical Insight

Tension PTX is a clinical diagnosis. Immediate needle decompression at 2nd ICS, mid-clavicular line — do not wait for imaging.

Cardiomegaly
CTR >0.5 · LVF · Pericardial Effusion
▼
Cardiomegaly — A = cardiac width, B = thoracic width. CTR = A÷B > 0.5

Definition

  • CTR > 0.5 on an erect PA chest X-ray

Associated Features in Cardiac Failure

  • Upper lobe vascular diversion — earliest sign of raised left atrial pressure
  • Kerley B lines — short horizontal lines at bases — interstitial oedema
  • Bat-wing opacity — perihilar — alveolar pulmonary oedema
  • Bilateral pleural effusions — late cardiac failure
Clinical Insight

On AP film: "Heart appears enlarged — technical magnification cannot be excluded on this AP projection."

CONSOLIDATION vs COLLAPSE vs EFFUSION
FeatureConsolidationCollapseEffusion
OpacityYesYesYes
Volume lossNoYesNo
Air bronchogramsYesNoNo
Mediastinal shiftNoneTowardAway
Meniscus signNoNoYes
